# PocketBase View Collection for User Post Counts

## Background

PocketBase supports View Collections, which are read-only collections backed by a custom SQLite query. This allows you to create aggregated views of your data, such as counting the number of related records.

## Requirements

* Initialize a PocketBase application.
* Create a `posts` collection with an `author` relation field pointing to the built-in `users` collection.
* Create a View Collection named `user_post_counts` that joins the `users` and `posts` collections.
* The `user_post_counts` view must return the user's `id`, their `username`, and a `post_count` representing the total number of posts they have authored.
* The schema of the view should include `id` (relation to users or text), `username` (text), and `post_count` (number).

## Implementation Hints

* Use PocketBase's migration system to define the schema for both the `posts` collection and the `user_post_counts` view collection.
* The SQLite query for the view should select from `users`, left join `posts` on the author relation, and group by `users.id`.
* Remember that View Collections require a unique `id` column for each row in the result set.
* You can write a Go migration (`pb_migrations/*.go`) or a JSON migration (`pb_migrations/*.js`) to define the collections programmatically.

## Acceptance Criteria

* Project path: /home/user/myproject
* Start command: ./pocketbase serve --http="0.0.0.0:8090"
* Port: 8090
* Collections:
  * A `posts` collection exists with an `author` field (type: relation, target: `users`).
  * A `user_post_counts` view collection exists.
* API Endpoints:
  * GET `/api/collections/user_post_counts/records`: Returns status 200 and a JSON array of records containing `id`, `username`, and `post_count`.
I'll start by exploring the working directory and understanding the project structure, then implement the PocketBase application with the required collections.

# Mental model for migrations #1947 - GitHub
created: 2023-03-01, last updated: 2026-04-18, [Read more](https://github.com/pocketbase/pocketbase/discussions/1947)

Yes.
`serve` will run all unapplied migrations from both the `pb_migrations` directory and the ones that are embedded in the binary.
The `--automigrate` flag is only for the automatic migration creation, for example when doing changes from the UI.
...
Yes.
All applied migrations are stored in the `_migrations` table.
`migrate down` will revert the last applied migration(s).
...
It is safe to run `pocketbase migrate collections` as many times as you want.
It creates a full snapshot of all collections configuration but keep in mind that if another developer has also created migration with a full snapshot, on merge you'll have to decide which version to keep since the last snapshot state will overwrite any previous one.
...
There is no clean/replace command at the moment and you'll have to delete manually the entries from the `_migrations` table if you want to do that.
I've added it to the roadmap to consider implementing a "migrate clean/sync" or similar command in the next release that will delete all missing previously applied migrations from the `_migrations` table.

created: 2025-11-30, last updated: 2025-12-03, [Read more](https://pocketbase.fr/migrations-pocketbase-js-go/)

Elles sont stockées dans un dossier dédié (
`pb_migrations` pour JS,
`migrations` pour Go) et trackées dans une table interne
`_migrations`.
...
- Option : Pour snapshot une collection entière :
`pocketbase migrate collections "snapshot_clients"`.
...
migrate(
(app) => {
let collection = new Collection({
type: "auth",
name: "clients",
listRule: "id = @request.auth.id",
viewRule: "id = @request.auth.id",
fields: [
{ type: "text", name: "company", required: true, max: 100 },
{ name: "website", type: "url", presentable: true }
],
passwordAuth: { enabled: false }, // Utilisez OTP
otp: { enabled: true },
...
});
app.save(collection);
...
**Go** : Utilisez
`core.NewAuthCollection("clients")`, ajoutez des fields avec
`*core.TextField{}`, et
`collection.AddIndex()`.
Pour un snapshot :
`migrate collections "clients_snapshot"` – éditez pour
`true` si vous voulez supprimer les absents.

# Introduction - Docs - PocketBase
created: undefined, last updated: 2026-06-12, [Read more](https://pocketbase.io/docs/)

Once you've extracted the archive, you could start the application by running
`**./pocketbase serve**` in the extracted directory.
...
The prebuilt PocketBase executable will create and manage 2 new directories alongside the executable:
- `pb_data` - stores your application data, uploaded files, etc. (usually should be added in
  `.gitignore`).
- `pb_migrations` - contains JS migration files with your collection changes (can be safely
  committed in your repository).
  You can even write custom migration scripts. For more info check the
  JS migrations docs.

# View Collection SQL Query #3873 - GitHub
created: 2023-12-03, last updated: 2026-04-16, [Read more](https://github.com/pocketbase/pocketbase/discussions/3873)

Root level `UNION` selects are not supported (see #1938).
An alternative, could be to wrap your query in a subselect, aka.
`SELECT id, username FROM ( ... )`
...
*Side-note about "I have two Auth Collections with same schema..."
- if all fields are the same you may find it easier to keep only 1 auth collection with a `select` "type" field or similar to differentiate between "clients" and "coaches"*
...
No, there shouldn't be any significant difference.
The only drawback of this I think is that the `username` will be treated as `json` field type instead of `text` since it will be an expression and because of some internal optimizations if you have a numeric username value it may get serialized as number.
To force always a string value you can try to cast it, something like:
```
SELECT id, CAST(username as text) as username
FROM (
SELECT id, username FROM coaches
UNION SELECT id, username FROM clients
)
```

# View collections - PocketBase
created: undefined, last updated: 2026-06-01, [Read more](https://pocketbase-pocketbase.mintlify.app/collections/view-collections)

SELECT
users.id,
users.email,
users.username,
COUNT(DISTINCT posts.id) as total_posts,
COUNT(DISTINCT comments.id) as total_comments,
MAX(posts.created) as last_post_date
FROM users
LEFT JOIN posts ON posts.author = users.id
LEFT JOIN comments ON comments.author = users.id
GROUP BY users.id
`
...
The query **must** include an `id` column.
If your query doesn't naturally have one, use SQL to create it (e.g., `ROW_NUMBER()` or alias an existing unique column).
...
Every view must have an `id` column for PocketBase to identify records.
...
-- Creating an ID from multiple columns
SELECT 
        (users.id || '_' || posts.id) as id,
users.name,
posts.title
FROM users
JOIN posts ON posts.author = users.id
```

products.id,
products.name,

products.price,
COALESCE(SUM(stock.quantity), 0) as total_stock,
COUNT(DISTINCT warehouses.id) as warehouse_count,
CASE 
            WHEN COALESCE(SUM(stock.quantity), 0) = 0 THEN 'out_of_stock'

# Collections
created: 2022-01-01, last updated: 2026-06-14, [Read more](https://pocketbase.io/docs/collections/)

Under the hood they are backed by plain SQLite tables that are generated automatically with the collection **name** and **fields** (columns).
...
##### View collection
**View collection** is a read-only collection type where the data is populated from a plain SQL
```
SELECT
```
statement, allowing users to perform aggregations or any other custom queries in
general.
For example, the following query will create a read-only collection with 3*posts* fields - *id*, *name* and *totalComments*:
```
SELECT
posts.id,
posts.name,
count(comments.id) as totalComments
FROM posts
LEFT JOIN comments on comments.postId = posts.id
GROUP BY posts.id
```
...
Dates are compared as strings, meaning that when using the filters with a date field you'll
have to specify the full datetime string format.
For example to target a single day (e.g.
November 19, 2024) you can use something like:
```
created >= '2024-11-19 00:00:00.000Z' && created <= '2024-11-19 23:59:59.999Z'
```

# Collections - PocketBase
created: undefined, last updated: 2026-06-15, [Read more](https://pocketbase-pocketbase.mintlify.app/go/collections)

* **base** - Standard collections for general data storage
* **auth** - Collections with built-in authentication features
* **view** - Read-only collections based on SQL queries
...
### NewViewCollection()
Create a view collection:
```go theme={null}
view := core.NewViewCollection("posts_stats")
view.ViewQuery = "SELECT id, COUNT(*) as total FROM posts GROUP BY category"
```
...
## View collection options
View collections are based on SQL queries:
```go theme={null}
view := core.NewViewCollection("user_stats")
view.ViewQuery = `
SELECT
users.id,
users.email,
COUNT(posts.id) as total_posts
FROM users
LEFT JOIN posts ON posts.user = users.id
GROUP BY users.id
`
```
<Warning>
View queries must include an `id` column.
Wildcard columns are not supported to prevent accidentally exposing sensitive data.
</Warning>

`CollectionQuery()` method.
It returns a SELECT DB builder that can be used with the same methods described in the Database guide.
...

...
// core.NewAuthCollection("example")
// core.NewViewCollection("example")
collection := core.NewBaseCollection("example")
// set rules
collection.ViewRule = types.Pointer("@request.auth.id != ''")
collection.CreateRule = types.Pointer("@request.auth.id != '' && @request.body.user = @request.auth.id")
collection.UpdateRule = types.Pointer(`
@request.auth.id != '' &&
user = @request.auth.id &&
(@request.body.user:isset = false || @request.body.user = @request.auth.id)
`)
...

titleField.Min = 10
...

...

if err != nil {
return err
}`

# Extended sql queries support. · pocketbase pocketbase · Discussion #1732
created: 2023-01-31, last updated: 2026-04-17, [Read more](https://github.com/pocketbase/pocketbase/discussions/1732)

```
SELECT
distinct(wi.warehouse_id) as WHID,
COUNT(wi.*) as MATCHES
FROM WAREHOUSE_ITEM as wi
WHERE wi.item_id IN (1,3,4,5)
GROUP BY (WHID)
ORDER BY MATCHES DESC
```
…
Each collection is represented as a plain SQL table with the schema fields as columns + some other system fields (eg. `id`, `created`, `updated`).
Depending on the "Max select" option of your relation field, the relation value could be stored as a plain id string OR a serialized json array of ids (aka. the same as the JSON representation in the response).
...

# Collections - PocketBase - Mintlify
created: 2026-03-03, last updated: 2026-04-14, [Read more](https://mintlify.wiki/pocketbase/pocketbase/concepts/collections)

### ​ View collections
View collections are read-only collections based on SQL queries.
They allow you to create virtual collections from complex queries without storing duplicate data.
```
collection := core.NewViewCollection("active_users")
collection.ViewQuery = "SELECT id, email FROM users WHERE verified = true"
```
**Limitations:** - Read-only (cannot create, update, or delete records)
- Fields are auto-generated from the query result
- Cannot have file fields
...
### ​ Querying collections
```
// Find by name
collection, err := app.FindCollectionByNameOrId("articles")
// Find by ID
collection, err := app.FindCachedCollectionByNameOrId("pbc_1234567890")
// List all collections
collections, err := app.FindAllCollections("")
// List only auth collections
authCollections, err := app.FindAllCollections(core.CollectionTypeAuth)
```
...
collection, err := app.FindCollectionByNameOrId("articles")
if err != nil {
return err
...
// Modify collection
collection.ListRule = types.Pointer("@request.auth.verified = true")
// Save changes
if err := app.Save(collection); err != nil {
return err
}

part.text: Now I have a clear picture. Let me also look at the PocketBase source to understand the exact structure for relation fields and view collections in v0.31:

content.text:
Title: Collections - PocketBase

URL Source: https://pocketbase-pocketbase.mintlify.app/go/collections

Markdown Content:
Collections define the structure of your data in PocketBase. Each collection has a type, fields, and various configuration options.

## Collection types

PocketBase supports three collection types:

*   **base** - Standard collections for general data storage
*   **auth** - Collections with built-in authentication features
*   **view** - Read-only collections based on SQL queries

## Creating collections

### NewCollection()

Create a new collection with the specified type:

```
collection := core.NewCollection(core.CollectionTypeBase, "posts")
```

### NewBaseCollection()

Create a base collection:

```
collection := core.NewBaseCollection("articles")
```

### NewAuthCollection()

Create an auth collection:

```
users := core.NewAuthCollection("users")
```

### NewViewCollection()

Create a view collection:

```
view := core.NewViewCollection("posts_stats")
view.ViewQuery = "SELECT id, COUNT(*) as total FROM posts GROUP BY category"
```

## Finding collections

### FindCollectionByNameOrId()

Find a collection by name or ID:

```
collection, err := app.FindCollectionByNameOrId("posts")
if err != nil {
    log.Fatal(err)
}
```

### FindCachedCollectionByNameOrId()

Find a collection from the app cache (faster, but read-only):

```
collection, err := app.FindCachedCollectionByNameOrId("posts")
if err != nil {
    log.Fatal(err)
}
```

### FindAllCollections()

Find all collections, optionally filtered by type:

```
// All collections
allCollections, err := app.FindAllCollections()

// Only auth collections
authCollections, err := app.FindAllCollections(core.CollectionTypeAuth)

// Auth and view collections
collections, err := app.FindAllCollections(
    core.CollectionTypeAuth,
    core.CollectionTypeView,
)
```

## Collection properties

### Basic properties

```
collection.Id          // Auto-generated ID
collection.Name        // Collection name (also the table name)
collection.Type        // base, auth, or view
collection.System      // System collection (cannot be deleted/renamed)
collection.Created     // Creation timestamp
collection.Updated     // Last update timestamp
```

### Type checking

```
if collection.IsBase() {
    log.Println("This is a base collection")
}

if collection.IsAuth() {
    log.Println("This is an auth collection")
}

if collection.IsView() {
    log.Println("This is a view collection")
}
```

## Managing fields

### Adding fields

```
collection.Fields.Add(
    &core.TextField{
        Name: "title",
        Required: true,
        Max: 200,
    },
    &core.TextField{
        Name: "content",
    },
    &core.NumberField{
        Name: "views",
        Min: types.Pointer(0.0),
    },
)
```

### Getting fields

```
// Get field by name
field := collection.Fields.GetByName("title")

// Get field by ID
field = collection.Fields.GetById("field_id")

// Get all field names
names := collection.Fields.FieldNames()
```

### Removing fields

```
collection.Fields.RemoveById("field_id")
collection.Fields.RemoveByName("old_field")
```

## API rules

Collections have API rules that control access:

```
// Allow anyone to list records
collection.ListRule = types.Pointer("")

// Only authenticated users can view individual records
collection.ViewRule = types.Pointer("@request.auth.id != ''")

// Only the record owner can update
collection.UpdateRule = types.Pointer("@request.auth.id = id")

// Only admins can delete
collection.DeleteRule = nil // nil means no access

// Anyone can create
collection.CreateRule = types.Pointer("")
```

## Auth collection options

Auth collections have additional configuration:

```
users := core.NewAuthCollection("users")

// Auth rule (who can authenticate)
users.AuthRule = types.Pointer("verified = true")

// OAuth2 providers
users.OAuth2.Providers = []core.OAuth2ProviderConfig{
    {
        Name: "google",
        ClientId: "your-client-id",
        ClientSecret: "your-client-secret",
        Enabled: true,
    },
}

// Password options
users.PasswordAuth.Enabled = true
users.PasswordAuth.IdentityFields = []string{"email", "username"}

// MFA options
users.MFA.Enabled = true
users.MFA.Duration = 1800 // 30 minutes

// OTP options
users.OTP.Enabled = true
users.OTP.Duration = 300 // 5 minutes
```

## View collection options

View collections are based on SQL queries:

```
view := core.NewViewCollection("user_stats")

view.ViewQuery = `
    SELECT
        users.id,
        users.email,
        COUNT(posts.id) as total_posts
    FROM users
    LEFT JOIN posts ON posts.user = users.id
    GROUP BY users.id
`
```

## Indexes

### Adding indexes

```
// Create a unique index
collection.AddIndex(
    "idx_email",
    true, // unique
    "`email`",
    "", // no WHERE clause
)

// Create a composite index with WHERE clause
collection.AddIndex(
    "idx_active_users",
    false, // not unique
    "`status`, `created`",
    "`status` = 'active'",
)
```

### Getting indexes

```
idx := collection.GetIndex("idx_email")
if idx != "" {
    log.Println("Index found:", idx)
}
```

### Removing indexes

```
collection.RemoveIndex("idx_email")
```

## Saving collections

### Save a new collection

```
collection := core.NewBaseCollection("posts")

collection.Fields.Add(
    &core.TextField{Name: "title", Required: true},
    &core.TextField{Name: "content"},
)

if err := app.Save(collection); err != nil {
    log.Fatal(err)
}
```

### Update an existing collection

```
collection, err := app.FindCollectionByNameOrId("posts")
if err != nil {
    log.Fatal(err)
}

collection.ListRule = types.Pointer("")

if err := app.Save(collection); err != nil {
    log.Fatal(err)
}
```

## Deleting collections

```
collection, err := app.FindCollectionByNameOrId("posts")
if err != nil {
    log.Fatal(err)
}

if err := app.Delete(collection); err != nil {
    log.Fatal(err)
}
```

## Collection references

Find all collections that reference a specific collection:

```
refs, err := app.FindCollectionReferences(collection)
if err != nil {
    log.Fatal(err)
}

for refCollection, fields := range refs {
    log.Printf("Collection %s references via fields:", refCollection.Name)
    for _, field := range fields {
        log.Printf("  - %s", field.GetName())
    }
}
```

## Importing collections

Import collections from configuration data:

```
collectionsData := []map[string]any{
    {
        "name": "posts",
        "type": "base",
        "fields": []map[string]any{
            {"name": "title", "type": "text"},
        },
    },
}

err := app.ImportCollections(collectionsData, false)
if err != nil {
    log.Fatal(err)
}
```

## Type signature

```
type Collection struct {
    Id         string
    Name       string
    Type       string
    System     bool
    Fields     FieldsList
    Indexes    types.JSONArray[string]
    ListRule   *string
    ViewRule   *string
    CreateRule *string
    UpdateRule *string
    DeleteRule *string
    Created    types.DateTime
    Updated    types.DateTime
}
```

content.text:
# Class RelationField - PocketBase JSVM reference
created: undefined, last updated: 2026-05-03, [Read more](https://pocketbase.io/jsvm/classes/RelationField.html)

RelationField defines "relation" type field for storing single or
multiple collection record references.
Requires the CollectionId option to be set.
If MaxSelect is not set or <= 1, then the field value is expected to be a single record id.
...
The respective zero record field value is either empty string (single) or empty string slice (multiple).
...
### Properties
cascadeDelete collectionId help hidden id maxSelect minSelect name presentable required system
…
...
## Properties
### cascade Delete
cascadeDelete: boolean
CascadeDelete indicates whether the root model should be deleted
in case of delete of all linked relations.
### collection Id
collectionId: string
CollectionId is the id of the related collection.
…
### id
id: string
Id is the unique stable field identifier.
It is automatically generated from the name when adding to a collection FieldsList.
### max Select
maxSelect: number
MaxSelect indicates the max number of allowed relation records
that could be linked to the main model.
For multiple select the value must be > 1, otherwise fallbacks to single (default).
If MinSelect is set, MaxSelect must be at least >= MinSelect.

# Relation field - PocketBase
created: undefined, last updated: 2026-06-07, [Read more](https://pocketbase-pocketbase.mintlify.app/fields/relation)

<ParamField path="collectionId" type="string" required>
The ID of the related collection.
This cannot be changed after the field is created.
</ParamField>
<ParamField path="cascadeDelete" type="bool" default="false">
When true, deletes the current record if all its linked relations are deleted.
</ParamField>
...
<Warning>
The `collectionId` cannot be changed after the field is created to prevent breaking existing data relationships.
...
field := &core.RelationField{
Name:         "author",
Required:     true,
MaxSelect:    1,
CollectionId: "users_collection_id",
}
...
Name:         "tags",
...
</Tab>
<Tab title="With cascade delete">
```go theme={null}
field := &core.RelationField{
Name:          "project",
Required:      true,
MaxSelect:     1,
CollectionId:  "projects_collection_id",
CascadeDelete: true, // Delete task when project is deleted
}
...
* Use `cascadeDelete` carefully as it can lead to unintended data loss
...
* Remember that changing `collectionId` is not allowed after creation

# Introduction - Working with relations - Docs - PocketBase
created: 2022-01-01, last updated: 2026-06-14, [Read more](https://pocketbase.io/docs/working-with-relations/)

The `relation` fields follow the same rules as any other collection field and can be set/modified
by directly updating the field value - with a record id or array of ids, in case a multiple relation is used.
...
To prepend/append a single or multiple relation id(s) to an existing value you can use the
`+` field modifier:
...
### Remove from multiple relation
To remove a single or multiple relation id(s) from an existing value you can use the
`-` field modifier:
`import PocketBase from 'pocketbase';...
const post = await pb.collection('posts').update('POST_ID', {
// remove single tag
'tags-': 'TAG_ID1',
// remove multiple tags at once
'tags-': ['TAG_ID1', 'TAG_ID2'],
})`...// remove single tag
'tags-': 'TAG_ID1',
// remove multiple tags at once
'tags-': ['TAG_ID1', 'TAG_ID2'],
})`
...
You can also expand record relation fields directly in the returned response without making additional
requests by using the `expand` query parameter, e.g. `?expand=user,post.tags`

# core package - github.com/pocketbase/pocketbase/core
created: undefined, last updated: 2026-05-24, [Read more](https://pkg.go.dev/github.com/pocketbase/pocketbase/core)

// // Note that this method will also trigger the records related // cascade and file delete actions.
...
FindCollectionReferences returns information for all relation fields referencing the provided collection.
If the provided collection has reference to itself then it will be also included in the result.
To exclude it, pass the collection id as the excludeIds argument.
...
RemoveById removes a single field by its id.
...
CollectionId string `form:"collectionId" json:"collectionId"` // CascadeDelete indicates whether the root model should be deleted // in case of delete of all linked relations.
CascadeDelete bool `form:"cascadeDelete" json:"cascadeDelete"` // MinSelect indicates the min number of allowed relation records // that could be linked to the main model.
…
RelationField defines "relation" type field for storing single or multiple collection record references.
Requires the CollectionId option to be set.
If MaxSelect is not set or <= 1, then the field value is expected to be a single record id.
If MaxSelect is > 1, then the field value is expected to be a slice of record ids.
The respective zero record field value is either empty string (single) or empty string slice (multiple).

# Fields reference - PocketBase
created: undefined, last updated: 2026-05-28, [Read more](https://pocketbase-pocketbase.mintlify.app/collections/fields)

## Relation field
Links to records in other collections.
```go theme={null}
&core.RelationField{
Name:          "author",
Required:      true,
CollectionId:  "users_collection_id",
MaxSelect:     1,
MinSelect:     1,
CascadeDelete: false,
}
```
### Properties
* **CollectionId** - ID of the related collection (required)
* **MaxSelect** - Max related records (1 = single, >1 = multiple)
* **MinSelect** - Min required relations
* **CascadeDelete** - Delete record if all relations are deleted
* **Required** - Require at least one relation
<Warning>
**CollectionId cannot be changed** after the field is created.
You must delete and recreate the field to change the target collection.
</Warning>

// Remove relations

Good. Now I need to create the Go migration files. However, PocketBase 0.31 uses Go migrations that need to be compiled into the binary. Since we're using the prebuilt `pocketbase` binary, we can't use Go migrations directly — we need to use JavaScript migrations (which are supported by the prebuilt binary).

Wait, let me re-check. The prebuilt binary supports both JS and Go migrations? Let me verify:

input.query: pocketbase prebuilt binary pb_migrations javascript go migration support

content.text:
# Extend with JavaScript - Migrations - Docs - PocketBase
created: undefined, last updated: 2026-06-12, [Read more](https://pocketbase.io/docs/js-migrations/)

Migrations
PocketBase comes with a builtin DB and data migration utility, allowing you to version your DB structure,
create collections programmatically, initialize default settings and/or run anything that needs to be
executed only once.
The user defined migrations are located in `pb_migrations` directory (it can be changed using
the
`--migrationsDir` flag) and each unapplied migration inside it will be executed automatically
in a transaction on `serve` (or on `migrate up`).
...
The prebuilt executable has the `--automigrate` flag enabled by default, meaning that every collection
configuration change from the Dashboard (or Web API) will generate the related migration file automatically
for you.
...
To create a new blank migration you can run `migrate create`.
...
migrate((app) => {
...
})`
New migrations are applied automatically on `serve`.
Optionally, you could apply new migrations manually by running `migrate up`.
...
The `migrate collections` command generates a full snapshot of your current collections
configuration without having to type it manually.
Similar to the `migrate create` command, this
will generate a new migration file in the
`pb_migrations` directory.

# Mental model for migrations #1947 - GitHub
created: 2023-03-01, last updated: 2026-04-18, [Read more](https://github.com/pocketbase/pocketbase/discussions/1947)

Yes.
`serve` will run all unapplied migrations from both the `pb_migrations` directory and the ones that are embedded in the binary.
The `--automigrate` flag is only for the automatic migration creation, for example when doing changes from the UI.
...
Yes.
All applied migrations are stored in the `_migrations` table.
`migrate down` will revert the last applied migration(s).
...
PocketBase migrations flow is similar to the other common frameworks/orms that you can find in Laravel, Django, etc.
If you delete the old migration files your app will still continue to work, but you'll not be able to revert beyond the point of the last deleted previously applied migration.

# How to do Migration when using pocket base as framework? · pocketbase pocketbase · Discussion #2218
created: 2023-04-05, last updated: 2026-04-16, [Read more](https://github.com/pocketbase/pocketbase/discussions/2218)

Please check https://pocketbase.io/docs/migrations/#enable-go-migrations.
You don't have to deploy the `migrations` directory when using Go migrations, because they will be embedded in the final executable.
...
The example main.go file uses JS migrations because the migrations are external, aka.
not part of the executable.
If you are using PocketBase as framework you can use directly the Go API.
Once again, please make sure to check https://pocketbase.io/docs/migrations/#enable-go-migrations.
...
Please note that the `example/base/main.go` is used for the prebuilt executable.
You don't have to copy it 1:1.
The minimal PocketBase `main.go` could be found in https://pocketbase.io/docs/use-as-framework/.
If you still for some reason want to load JS migration for your custom binary, you have to enable also the `jsvm` migrations plugin as shown in https://github.com/pocketbase/pocketbase/blob/master/examples/base/main.go#L70-L73.
But again, for most cases this shouldn't be necessary.
Please make sure to read https://pocketbase.io/docs/migrations/#enable-go-migrations.

# Introduction - Docs - PocketBase
created: undefined, last updated: 2026-06-12, [Read more](https://pocketbase.io/docs/)

The easiest way to get started is to download the prebuilt minimal PocketBase executable:
- Download v0.39.3 for Linux x64 (~12MB zip)
- Download v0.39.3 for Windows x64 (~12MB zip)
- Download v0.39.3 for macOS x64 (~12MB zip)
- Download v0.39.3 for Linux ARM64 (~11MB zip)
- Download v0.39.3 for Windows ARM64 (~11MB zip)
- Download v0.39.3 for macOS ARM64 (~11MB zip)
...
Once you've extracted the archive, you could start the application by running
`**./pocketbase serve**` in the extracted directory.
...
The prebuilt PocketBase executable will create and manage 2 new directories alongside the executable:
- `pb_data` - stores your application data, uploaded files, etc. (usually should be added in
  `.gitignore`).
- `pb_migrations` - contains JS migration files with your collection changes (can be safely
  committed in your repository).
  You can even write custom migration scripts. For more info check the
  JS migrations docs.

# database structure update · pocketbase pocketbase · Discussion #1247
created: 2022-12-12, last updated: 2026-04-16, [Read more](https://github.com/pocketbase/pocketbase/discussions/1247)

You can create a migration.
With v0.9+ there is support for "automigration" that will create a `pb_migrations` directory that you can commit safely to your repo.
The migration files can be both Go or JS (for the latter documentation will be available soon).
Migrations are automatically applied on `serve`.
...
Do you have automigrate enabled?
If yes, then when making collection changes from the UI, a migration file(s) will be created for you and they will be applied automatically on server start.
...
`migrate create` creates a **blank** migration that you have to fill with your specific changes manually.
...
It should be already enabled by default.
If not (it is possible that on windows the tmp dir check may not be reliable), start the executable with `--automigrate=1`.
But in all cases you can trigger a full collections snapshot by running `migrate collections` (when automigrate is enabled for the first time it will actually create a full snapshot as a first migration to ensure that after this point a more granular migration files are created).
...
- make sure that both environments has the same `pb_migrations` files content.
...
- -

# Can I extend PocketBase with both JS and Go? - Reddit
created: 2025-08-05, last updated: 2026-05-08, [Read more](https://www.reddit.com/r/pocketbase/comments/1miezkr/can_i_extend_pocketbase_with_both_js_and_go/)

Absolutely, we are implementing that.
You can view the official example at this link:  
://github/pocket/pocket/blob/master/examples/base/main.go.
Just fork the repository and begin incorporating your own Go code as you would in any standard Go project.
All your current JavaScript hooks and endpoints will function seamlessly alongside it.
We've successfully operated both together while utilizing the same database without any problems.

# Extend with Go - Migrations - Docs - PocketBase
created: undefined, last updated: 2026-06-15, [Read more](https://pocketbase.io/docs/go-migrations/)

Migrations
PocketBase comes with a builtin DB and data migration utility, allowing you to version your DB structure,
create collections programmatically, initialize default settings, etc.
Because the migrations are regular Go functions, besides applying schema changes, they could be used also
to adjust existing data to fit the new schema or any other app specific logic that you want to run only
once.
And as a bonus, being `.go` files also ensure that the migrations will be embedded seamlessly in
your final executable.
...
*You can find all available config options in the
`migratecmd` subpackage.*
...
To create a new blank migration you can run `migrate create`.
...
package migrations
...
Load migrations
To make your application aware of the registered migrations, you have to import the above
`migrations` package in one of your `main` package files:
`package main
import _ "yourpackage/migrations"
// ...`
...
New unapplied migrations are automatically executed when the application server starts, aka. on
`serve`.
Alternatively, you can also apply new migrations manually by running `migrate up`.
To revert the last applied migration(s), you can run `migrate down [number]`.

# \* Pocketbase Update */ · pocketbase pocketbase · Discussion #1126
created: 2022-11-26, last updated: 2025-06-07, [Read more](https://github.com/pocketbase/pocketbase/discussions/1126)

**Please note that PocketBase is still under active development and backward compatibility is not guaranteed before reaching v1 and therefore is not suitable at the moment for critical production applications, unless you are OK with putting an extra effort to perform manual migration steps from time to time.**
...
During development, if you use PocketBase as framework, you can write migration scripts with Go and embe…
...
During development, if you use PocketBase as framework, you can write migration scripts with Go and embed them in your final executable - https://pocketbase.io/docs/migrations/.
Migrations are automatically applied on server start.
I'm currently also experimenting with different approaches to allow similar experience when the prebuilt PocketBase executable is used and most likely with the next v0.9 release you'll be able to write migration scripts with JavaScript.
So to answer you question - when new PocketBase version is released, currently you'll have to:
- read the changelog and apply any necessary changes in your code
- upload the new binary (*at least on linux, you don't need to first stop the server when removing the old binary, because the os will keep the old inode until there is a process using it, so it is safe to call
...
rm
...
,
...
,

# Correct way to have development setup with continuous delivery · pocketbase pocketbase · Discussion #3748
created: 2023-11-14, last updated: 2026-04-17, [Read more](https://github.com/pocketbase/pocketbase/discussions/3748)

If you use PocketBase as Go framework, aka.
you have your own Go executable, then the migrations will be included in the resulting binary (to setup migrations with Go you could check the Go Migrations guide).
...
If you use the prebuilt executable, automigrate is enabled by default.
This means that in order to "transfer" you local schema changes to a remote instance, it is enough to copy the `pb_migrations` directory and restart the application (*unapplied migrations are automatically applied on `serve`*).
You can safely commit in git the `pb_migrations` directory to share it with others.
